text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang at Coherent, Sherman TX:

"AI runs on compute, but it scales with connectivity"

everyone's watching the GPU race - Jensen says the real bottleneck is something else entirely

→ connecting millions of chips across a data center requires light, not electricity. copper can't travel hundreds of feet fast enough. photonics - converting electricity to laser pulses - is what makes AI factories possible at scale
→ the full AI stack has five layers: energy grid, chips, infrastructure, models, applications. a breakthrough at any layer changes the economics of all the others
→ AI could be what finally makes sustainable energy fully market-driven - no subsidies needed for the first time in history. nuclear, solar, wind - all fundable by AI demand alone

bookmark this ↓

3. Infrastructure (AI Factories, Networking, Cloud)
The data centers, high-speed networking fabrics, cooling, and orchestration layers that turn individual chips into giant intelligent systems.
Retail edge: Networking and connectivity specialists (e.g., Broadcom, Arista, and related silicon photonics or connectivity names) often sit in a sweet spot — critical to scaling clusters but sometimes less crowded than pure GPU names. Data center power delivery and thermal management plays can also offer better entry points for patient capital.

Our fren @FundaAI posted in May about the 2.4T (300G lanes) adoption timelines. I found it very important, and I took good note.

Now professor Damnang is hearing the same in the street about it (remember he has contact with the main players in the Industry).

The higher technology bar needed in Photonics for 2.4T , in my view, will benefit the Big Players $LITE $COHR $AAOI etc. vs Tier 3 suppliers

Next big question is.... will delay fully CPO adoption? 🤔

https://t.co/MSW5Xft2Wp

text:
🚨The best deep dive I've seen on the optical chokepoint. Worth reading in full.

The core point is the one I keep coming back to → the constraint in AI optics isn't the GPU or the silicon photonics die. It's the III-V InP laser that silicon physically cannot produce, made in a handful of specialized fabs with 18 month lead times and qualification lock-in measured in years. Own the layer they're forced to buy. 🧐

How I'm positioned across the photonics stack:

🔵 $SIVEF → the merchant InP laser itself. The scarcest node, design-ins across Ayar, $POET $GFS ✅
🟠 $AAOI → the vertically integrated US player making its own lasers and modules end to end. ✅
🟠 $CRDO → one layer over → the connectivity and retiming that the optical lanes run through. (Copper → silicon opticals in-house with Dust Photonics acquisition)

The laser is the bottleneck. The stack around it is the trade. ⚡️

Thanks for sharing Nexus Finance work @StormDirac 

Long $SIVEF $AAOI $CRDO (3 of my largest holdings) 💪

text:
Anders, this is one of the best breakdowns of the laser bottleneck. The framing that silicon is the highway and InP is the headlights gets at the thing most photonics coverage misses → the chokepoint isn't the silicon chip everyone models, it's the III-V laser they can't make on it. 🧐

The M&A-as-validation read is on point too. Watching the acquirers tells you where the locked-in revenue lands two years out. Appreciate the depth here, this is the kind of work that actually moves the conversation forward. I am RTing it for all! Long $SIVE $SIVEF and $AAOI (2 of my largest positions!) 🤝

text: A strong bullish prediction on photonics - will there be a major reversal soon?
embedded_text:

I call that; an Opportunity.

We want to see a bounce at this support level to confirm the support/resistance flip.

2. $SIVE

text:
2026 PORTFOLIO UPDATE  Performance Metrics 

June 2026     :    6.39%
Harris YTD     : 45.79% 
S&P500 YTD: 12.84%  

My portfolio currently has 9 stocks: $nbis, $sofi, $mu, $aaoi, $asts, $oust, $pltr, $nflx, $onds

Currently 6/9 stocks in green, lagging only $sofi (who else!), $aaoi and $nflx.

Month Recap: It wasn't an easy month. It had a lot of ups and downs, and I'm really surprised that we closed the month in green. At one point I was -9%, but with the huge pump the last few days of the month, we managed to close in profit.

I will write down some thoughts of mine per each holding.
$nbis: This is the stock I have high conviction on. That doesn't mean I didn't trade it. I sold and bought quite a lot of times to make some profits and to initiate other positions. I've held it for one year now.
$sofi: I hold it from last year. Still in red, but I managed to bring my average down to $18.7, so I'm pretty close to breaking even. I love their CEO (@anthonynoto, send me a hat) as I believe he makes an extremely good job. The company is progressing really well, and the stock price will follow eventually.
$mu: I used to have SK Hynix, and I switched to have exposure to US markets. That's all. Same philosophy. Memory is in deep need.
$aaoi: A big player in photonics. All sector had a bad month. It will recover (I'm -14%), I'm not afraid.
$asts: I started buying when $spcx launched all the way to the bottom. I'm in green, I have a good average, and I believe a lot. Nothing more to say.
$oust: You see what happened last days with Physical AI and robots. Initially I bought in Aprin around $23 and sold at $36 for quick profit. Now I'm long here. Huge profit atm, I expect a pullback to take a breather.
$pltr & $nflx: I wanted exposure to both companies and I was looking for the opportunity. When $pltr dropped below 200week ema, I bought it and I have averaged. Netflix (last stock in red) gives me more opportunities for DCAing.
$onds: It was on my radar. I have issues with their CEO and his tactics (dilution and pumping), but at $7.9 it was a bargain and there was a huge disconnect between the company and the stock price. It's not a long-term position. When it reaches $10-$12, I planned to trim it.

Thanks for staying up to the end! If you enjoyed it, drop a 👋 emoji to see who read it.

Consider following me in order to be together in that journey. Today I reached 100k portfolio, so that's a huge milestone to celebrate!

text:
Half of the year is overwith. 

I have been fairly inactive over the last month as I spend time reflecting on the year, analyzing my holdings and seeing new opportunities for 2H. 

If I compartmentalize the year by months, I outperformed the majority in months 1-3 by having a defensive portfolio 25’ that was primary legacy manufacturers like $vrt $cat $gev. From April -> May I slightly underperformed, by not being able to pivot fast enough into emerging themes like memory and semis, only getting exposure to photonics through $aaoi. 

May -> present was OK, although my heavy positioning in $spcx ancillaries pulled performance down. Looking ahead I see no reason to 2H 26’ to be any different than what we have seen for the last few years. 

As I’m writing this I see Burry is now short my second largest holding caterpillar and I kindly ask him to accept my ballbag as I maintain bullish on the resurgence of heavy machinery and manufacturing names amidst an extremely pro America/infrastructure administration.

text:
Here's the post:

If I could only watch four layers in optical interconnect, ranked by bottleneck severity:
$COHR $LITE $FN $ANET

Nvidia just spent $6 billion locking down optical supply between March and June, and the market still thinks this is about transceivers.

The real story is InP substrates and laser chips — the stuff 20 layers upstream from what most people are tracking. Here's how I'd rank the supply chain by choke point plus earnings visibility over the next 12-24 months.

1. InP materials and epitaxy — the actual valve on compute scale. $COHR and $LITE aren't just CPO plays. They control high-purity indium phosphide epitaxy and high-speed laser IP that physically cannot be replaced in 800G/1.6T modules. Intel's CEO said it outright: InP is the non-negotiable material for solving chip-to-chip bandwidth. Nvidia locked each of them with separate $2 billion commitments and long-term offtake agreements. The Street prices them as module vendors. They're actually material oligopolists with compounding pricing power through every speed iteration.

2. CPO laser engines — the leverage on every GPU watt. $LITE and $COHR again, but now wearing their co-packaged optics hat. CPO moves the photonics inside the package, right next to the switch ASIC or GPU. That requires advanced packaging plus high-power lasers that don't cook themselves. Supply is sewn up. Goldman just put the scale-up interconnect TAM at $154 billion, and most of that growth is inside the rack and inside the super-pod, not between data centers. CPO sits in the middle of that build-out. Earnings inflection is 2026-2028 when hyperscalers move from pilots to volume. Risk is simple: if Nvidia capex wobbles, these names will gap hard because the trade is already crowded.

3. Module assembly and high-mix manufacturing — where cash flow actually prints. $FN is the TSMC of optical: low-drama contract manufacturing for every major module and CPO vendor. Margins are thinner than the chip guys, but revenue visibility is the cleanest in the whole chain because hyperscale deployment schedules are locked 18 months out. TrendForce just lifted 2025-2026 AI transceiver revenue from $16.5 billion to $26 billion, up 57% year-over-year. FN captures that with zero demand risk and no inventory. It's the only name here where you can model the next eight quarters with a straight face.

4. Switch and networking integration — the layer everyone already owns. $ANET is the consensus optical AI trade. Data center switching tied to optical back-end is a known quantity. Multiples reflect it. I'd rather own the three above because the market hasn't priced the material and assembly bottleneck the same way it's priced the switch attach rate.

The non-consensus bit: A-share money has been rotating from NAND and legacy semis into InP names for two quarters, but U.S. allocators still think optical is a 2025 story. It's a 2026-2028 margin story, and the earnings gap between upstream material plays and downstream module integrators is about to widen hard. I'm overweight the top two on that list until CPO production data forces multiple expansion.

text:
This 🎯roadmap🎯 for data-center interconnect speeds is also, in many ways, a roadmap for #siliconphotonics.  As data rates move higher, the industry will need more integrated optical solutions, including near-package optics and co-packaged optics. That is where #siliconphotonics becomes increasingly relevant.
I adapted this roadmap based on analysis from an investment bank, which states that it is built on company disclosures as well as its own research.
It is interesting to observe how the upgrade cycles are overlapping. According to this analysis 400G is already moving into decline, 800G is becoming the mainstream workhorse, 1.6T is entering mass-volume territory, and 3.2T is already visible before 1.6T has fully settled. Furthermore, 800G may remain mainstream for only around six quarters. That is shorter than the typical 2–4 year cadence often expected for a mainstream optical transceiver generation. This points to a compressed innovation cadence for the industry. Qualification, yield learning, supply-chain readiness, thermal design, packaging, DSP, and test infrastructure all need to mature faster than before. Only the 💪fittest can survive this race. My view is that integrated photonics is entering a sustained innovation cycle, driven by the bandwidth, power, density, and packaging pressures of AI data-center infrastructure. 
🚀 #siliconphotonics

text: 6G's whole pitch rides on terahertz, and terahertz is brutal to actually build antennas for. text:
Russia ITMO University: Optical Tractor Beam with Metasurfaces and United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

2 - Within this report I present the top 3 companies I believe are well positioned. A quick thesis / overview

3 - Once I decide upon the top candidates, I then do a full deep dive into every aspect of the company, along with the weightings I am interested in and the financial modeling around them

While this is a very exciting time for this theme, we are still very early. There is no rush on my end for positioning or research. 

I started covering Physical AI ~6 weeks ago and along with photonics, these are the two themes I am researching and covering in depth. 

If we can really identify the strongest companies, we can position with a multi-year mindset

I still have 7 more layers to present and lots of positions I still need to take

Join us if you are interested!

text:
#truefreefinance  (June 27.2026 )Robotics is quietly emerging as the next major wave in tech investing
Recent data from Andreessen Horowitz and PitchBook (March update) suggests that investment activity in robotics is accelerating, with both funding and deal flow continuing to rise.
What makes this trend particularly interesting is its connection to the broader AI infrastructure cycle.
Many of the same sectors benefiting from AI data center expansion are also positioned to play a role in robotics and humanoid systems:
• Memory (DRAM & NAND) → supporting on-device AI inference and storage
• Photonics & optics → enabling machine vision, LiDAR, and sensing systems
• AI compute infrastructure → powering autonomous decision-making in real-world environments
At the moment, most exposure is still indirect — through upstream suppliers or large tech companies investing heavily in robotics, such as Amazon and Tesla.
But the bigger question for investors is forward-looking:
If capital markets reopen strongly in 2026–2027, will we start seeing more pure-play robotics and humanoid companies coming to public markets?
If that happens, robotics could move from a niche theme to a full investment cycle of its own.
Not financial advice. Based on publicly available market data and industry trends.
#AI #Robotics #HumanoidRobots #Technology #Investing #FutureTech #StockMarket

text:
Nvidia quietly became the #1 data center Ethernet switch vendor — $2.1B revenue, 21.5% market share, up from 4% two years ago. IDC calls it 'one of the most significant vendor landscape shifts' in enterprise networking history. The networking business tripled to $15B.

The obvious read: Nvidia is diversifying beyond GPUs.

The more strategic read: Nvidia is identifying the next AI bottleneck before it fully materializes, then buying or building the solution before anyone else realizes it's a problem.

At million-GPU cluster scale, the binding constraint isn't compute or even memory anymore. It's the fabric connecting them. Copper interconnects hit hard walls on power, bandwidth density, and reach. GPU utilization tanks when all-reduce and collective communication across racks can't keep up.

Every cluster operator has experienced this: plenty of FLOPs, terrible effective throughput because the network is the bottleneck.

Nvidia saw this coming when it bought Mellanox for $6.9B in 2019. Spectrum-X now generates more revenue than all Ethernet networking peers combined, per Jensen. The company is vertically integrating end-to-end cluster fabric the same way it integrated CUDA into the software stack.

The next phase: photonics. Nvidia's CPO roadmap with TSMC COUPE is preparing for optical interconnects at the rack level. Bandwidth per AI rack goes from ~130 TB/s to multiples beyond that.

Here's the under-covered part: the photonics supply chain — lasers, silicon photonics foundries, fiber attach, packaging — is even earlier in its high-volume ramp than HBM was in 2024. The qualification cycles are longer, yields lower, and the ecosystem less mature.

The AI bottleneck narrative has shifted: compute → memory → networking → optics.

$15B in networking revenue is the market signaling the next constraint. The companies that solve the photonics supply chain will be the Micron and SK Hynix of the next cycle.

#Nvidia #Photonics #AIChips #Networking

text:
Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs, while institutional money is quietly locking up the raw physical supply chains today.

The vertical spike highlighted confirms a massive paradigm shift: capital is moving aggressively into physical, embodied AI. As the first pure-play humanoid exits open the liquidity floodgates for H2 into 2027, the structural allocation war we are seeing in data centers will expand exponentially into robotics.

The smart money is front-running this cross-exposure overlap through critical upstream hardware:
 The Memory Squeeze Phase II: A humanoid robot running dense, real-time localized inference models requires up to a 10x memory multiplier over regular edge hardware. The exact same HBM, DRAM, and NAND components currently supply-constrained through 2027 to feed hyperscalers are facing an entirely new, massive downstream demand sink.
 The Photonics Vision Choke Point: You cannot run autonomous, real-time spatial manipulation on a gutted hardware spec sheet. Next-gen FMCW LiDAR vision and sensor frameworks rely directly on high-power DFB laser chips. Companies like $AAOI or $LITE that control internal MOCVD crystal growth foundries aren't just building for transceivers—they are the literal gatekeepers of the physical optical sensing layer that every single humanoid platform requires to operate safely.

The Upstream Component Leverage: Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs to buy the hype, completely missing that the absolute pricing power sits with the upstream component layer right now. Whether the end-program is run by $TSLA , $AMZN or a venture-backed pure-play, they all must secure raw, physical capacity from the exact same tightly contracted component suppliers.

The cognition bottleneck has officially shifted to a physical execution bottleneck. When the factories are fully booked through mid-2027 just to satisfy data center demand, adding a multi-billion dollar humanoid hardware ramp into the equation means the choke points capture all the structural alpha.

Accumulating the physical plumbing before the global IPO season reprices the entire supply chain. Long the bottlenecks. 📈🤖🧠

text:
🧵 My personal market heuristic after tracking AI infrastructure themes closely:

Markets tend to price things in ~8 months in advance — except Space, Robotics, and Quantum, where sentiment runs well ahead of fundamentals.

Here's how I'm applying it across themes I follow: 👇

Photonics has a clear roadmap:

• 1.6T pluggables ( $LITE, $COHR, $AAOI) are hitting now
• CPO Scale-Out ramp: late H2 2026
• CPO Scale-Up (Ayar, Celestial, etc.): H2 2027

By the 8M rule, CPO Scale-Up names are still ~4 months early to position heavily.

Memory is mid-bottleneck and re-rating accordingly:

• HBM3e: now ($MU, Samsung, SK Hynix)
• HBM4 mass production: ramping now
• HBF: 2027

Same players across cycles = compounding opportunity as each architecture matures.

Glass Substrates:

• SKC Absolics early ramp: H2 2026
• Volume inflection: H2 2027–2028
• TSMC CoPoS, Samsung, others: 2027–2028

Machine/equipment suppliers here tend to see revenue before the volume ramp — worth noting for positioning.

Neoclouds: Q4 2026 earnings looks like a key inflection window for leading players.

In hindsight, March 2026 was the entry, not September 2025. The ~8M rule would've caught that.

Current repositioning focus:
• Memory
• Neoclouds
• Photonics
• Robotics

Preference for names capturing multiple themes simultaneously (e.g., pluggable + NPO + CPO exposure) and tactical exposure to bottleneck plays like advanced packaging and MLCCs.

Key personal lesson: Avoid heavy exposure to names 12M+ out. Early conviction is fine — overweighting too early is where sizing discipline matters most.

Still learning. These are just my own frameworks — not advice. 🙏

text:
Reflections in the Dip: Why My Conviction in $SIVE Has Never Been Stronger

I’ve been watching the recent pullback with a strange sense of calm.

Weak hands are panicking. That’s what they do when the price moves against them for a few weeks. But if you’ve been here before, you know this feeling isn’t new, it’s just the market doing what it always does to test conviction.

Over two years ago we were trading around 1.8 SEK. That drop felt brutal at the time. This one? This is noise compared to that. And yet the company is in a completely different place today.

We’re still in an industry that hasn’t really started yet. Photonics for AI data centers is still pre-ramp for most people. There are no shiny consumer products on shelves for the average investor to point at and say “that’s what I own.” No flashy earnings beats every quarter. Just deep technology, long qualification cycles, and a market that very few truly understand. Most people want to see something they can touch before they believe. That’s why these moves feel so violent, because the narrative isn’t obvious until it suddenly is.

That’s also why wireless has been so important. It’s been the quiet engine running in the background, delivering real revenue and building the foundation while photonics gets ready for its moment. Most of the market still doesn’t price that in properly. They’re too focused on the “when does photonics happen?” question and miss that the company is already executing on multiple fronts.

I expect photonics news and orders from Ayar Labs, $POET, O-Net, $AEVA and $JBL before the year is out. Those won’t just be headlines, they’ll be real validation that starts to de-risk the story and reduce some of this volatility. I’m 100% convinced of that. Not because I’m hoping. Because I’ve followed this company long enough to know how it works.

Nobody believes until it’s too late.

We saw it from 4 SEK to over 100 SEK. Almost nobody saw that coming when it was actually happening. The next leg won’t be any different.

There’s a lot of games being played right now, short sellers, institutions, traders all trying to take their piece. That’s normal when a stock has moved this much. It will stop when they’re done. Right now we’re also at a price level where a lot of serious money starts paying attention again. Don’t let the noise fool you.

Do your own work.

Read the presentations. Watch the CMDs. Look at the customer pipeline, the partnerships, the technology edge. Build your own picture instead of scrolling through what everyone else is saying.

If you don’t believe light is the future of high-speed connectivity, that’s fine. There are plenty of other sectors. But if you do understand the technology and what Sivers actually brings to the table, the differentiated InP platform, the asset-light model, the real design wins, then the choice becomes pretty obvious.

This is still a market that very few people truly grasp. A market that doesn’t even fully know how big it can get. And if you play it right, it rewards patience like few others.
I actually believe more in $SIVEF today than I did a couple of years ago.

Why? Because I can see the moves that are coming. Dual listing preparations are real. They’re not walking onto Nasdaq empty-handed. Watch what happens over the next few months. A lot of people are going to wish they had listened earlier.

For what it’s worth, I haven’t sold a single share. And I won’t. The day I sell is the day the company stops delivering. Not when the stock has a bad week. Not when the noise gets loud. Price and company are two different things.

This is still the early innings.

Stay calm. Do the work. Trust what you know.

$AAOI +289% – Rode the AI/data‑center optics boom as hyperscalers upgraded networking gear.

$AEHR +355% – Benefited from surging demand for semiconductor test systems in EVs and advanced chips.

$ARM +206% – Capitalized on ARM‑based CPU adoption across smartphones, PCs and data‑center designs.

$AXTI +329% – Outperformed on strong orders for compound semiconductors used in 5G and photonics.

$BE +191% – Gained from investor enthusiasm for hydrogen and solid‑oxide fuel cell clean‑energy plays.

$DELL +213% – Surged on AI server and enterprise PC strength, plus aggressive capital returns.

$DOCN +190% – Rose with demand for developer‑friendly cloud infrastructure and higher‑margin services.

$INTC +248% – Re‑rated as investors backed its foundry turnaround and onshoring of advanced chipmaking.

$MRVL +214% – Rallied on its positioning in high‑speed networking and custom silicon for AI workloads.

$MU +297% – Exploded higher as DRAM/NAND prices recovered and AI memory demand spiked.

$NBIS +187% – Benefited from niche exposure to fast‑growing tech segments and improving fundamentals.

$PENG +218% – Climbed on strong traction in specialized software/services tied to digital infrastructure.

$SNDK +781% – Went parabolic as flash‑storage pricing and AI‑driven data growth supercharged earnings.

$TWST +215% – Advanced on breakthroughs in synthetic biology and growing demand for DNA writing/reading.

$WDC +240% – Ripped higher on HDD/flash turnaround and restructuring moves unlocking storage value.

text:
ちょうど昨日Zackの記事もありました、GFSとUMCの投資ロジックの違いについて

1. 成長ドライバー：シリコンフォトニクス vs. 22nm成熟プロセス
 GlobalFoundries (GFS) —— 高成長なニッチ市場への注力：
GFSはターゲットを絞ったアプローチをとっており、AIやデータセンターのインフラ需要を直接取り込んでいます。特に、光通信向けのシリコンフォトニクス (Silicon Photonics) 技術が牽引しており、2026年には同事業の売上が倍増、2028年末にはランレートで10億ドルを突破する見込みです。また、高周波通信向けのシリコンゲルマニウム (SiGe) ターンキーソリューションは2027年までキャパシティが埋まっています。AI光通信チェーンや欧米のローカルサプライチェーンのテーマに投資したい場合、GFSのストーリーは非常に魅力的です。
 UMC (聯電) —— 規模の経済とコスト効率：
UMCは成熟プロセスの雄であり、主力である22nmプラットフォームが業績を支えています。2026年末までに50社以上の顧客がテープアウト（設計完了）を予定しており、ディスプレイ駆動ICやマイクロコントローラ（MCU）など、幅広い汎用チップを網羅しています。GFSのような派手なAIインフラのテーマ性は薄いものの、Intelと共同開発している12nmプロセスが2027年に量産開始予定であり、将来的に高付加価値市場への参入ロードマップを描いています。

2. 財務パフォーマンスとバリュエーション
直近の2026年第1四半期（Q1）決算では、両社ともに売上総利益率（グロス・マージン）を約29%に維持していますが、その中身は異なります。
 GFS（バリュエーションは高め、長期的価格支配力）： スマートフォン市場の減速によりモバイル部門が逆風を受けているものの、デザインウィン（設計獲得）が前年同期比で50%急増しています。これは顧客の囲い込みが順調である証拠であり、長期的な価格支配力の強さを示しています。
 UMC（稼働率回復による短期的な利益爆発力）： Q1の設備稼働率が79%（前年同期から10ポイント上昇）まで回復したことで、営業レバレッジが効き、純利益が大幅に急増しました。また、UMCは伝統的に配当が安定しており配当利回りが高いため、インカムゲインを重視する投資家にとっても安心感があります。

3. どちらが今日の優れた投資機会か？
投資スタイルやポートフォリオの戦略によって結論が分かれます。
📥 GlobalFoundries (GFS) を選ぶべきケース：
長期的なアルファ（市場平均を超える超過リターン）を狙う場合です。
主力であるモバイル向けビジネスが2026年後半まで高一桁の減少を見込むなど短期的には逆風がありますが、米国・ドイツ・シンガポールに分散された製造拠点は、地政学的リスクを嫌う大手クライアント（Appleやルネサスなど）のニーズに合致しています。プレミアムなマルチプル（株価倍率）を許容し、AIインフラや光ネットワークへの構造転換に賭けたい投資家向けです。
📥 UMC (聯電) を選ぶべきケース：
景気サイクル好転による安心感と、確実なキャッシュフローを狙う場合です。
現在のUMCの主な懸念点は、シンガポール工場の拡張に伴う巨額の減価償却費であり、これが2026年後半の利益率を圧迫するリスクがあります。しかし、世界的な自動車、産業、家電向けチップの需要が本格的に回復すると予想する場合、稼働率上昇がそのまま利益に直結する高い財務レバレッジと、下値リスクを抑える高配当（防衛力）を兼ね備えた、バランスの良い循環株として非常に優秀です。

スマートフォン市場の減速によりモバイル部門が逆風を受けているものの、デザインウィン（設計獲得）が前年同期比で50%急増しています。これは顧客の囲い込みが順調である証拠であり、長期的な価格支配力の強さを示しています。 UMC（稼働率回復による短期的な利益爆発力）： Q1の設備稼働率が79%（前年同期から10ポイント上昇）まで回復したことで、営業レバレッジが効き、純利益が大幅に急増しました。また、UMCは伝統的に配当が安定しており配当利回りが高いため、インカムゲインを重視する投資家にとっても安心感があります。 3. どちらが今日の優れた投資機会か？ 投資スタイルやポートフォリオの戦略によって結論が分かれます。 📥 GlobalFoundries (GFS) を選ぶべきケース： 長期的なアルファ（市場平均を超える超過リターン）を狙う場合です。 主力であるモバイル向けビジネスが2026年後半まで高一桁の減少を見込むなど短期的には逆風がありますが、米国・ドイツ・シンガポールに分散された製造拠点は、地政学的リスクを嫌う大手クライアント（Appleやルネサスなど）のニーズに合致しています。プレミアムなマルチプル（株価倍率）を許容し、AIインフラや光ネットワークへの構造転換に賭けたい投資家向けです。 📥 UMC (聯電) を選ぶべきケース： 景気サイクル好転による安心感と、確実なキャッシュフローを狙う場合です。 現在のUMCの主な懸念点は、シンガポール工場の拡張に伴う巨額の減価償却費であり、これが2026年後半の利益率を圧迫するリスクがあります。しかし、世界的な自動車、産業、家電向けチップの需要が本格的に回復すると予想する場合、稼働率上昇がそのまま利益に直結する高い財務レバレッジと、下値リスクを抑える高配当（防衛力）を兼ね備えた、バランスの良い循環株として非常に優秀です。"}}}, "rest_id": text:
Q1 2026 just printed a record: $16.3B invested across ~492 deals in robotics & physical AI. Humanoids are eating a massive slice of that capital. 

Deal sizes in the hundreds of millions are now normal. The steep upward trend you highlighted is accelerating into deployment phase, not just demos.
The cross-exposure angle is one of the highest-conviction parts of this story:

Memory (DRAM/HBM + NAND): Humanoids need serious onboard inference + storage. Same AI data center stack that’s already in hyper-growth directly feeds edge humanoid brains.

Photonics & sensing (DFB lasers for FMCW LiDAR): This is the vision/perception layer that turns clunky robots into dexterous, safe collaborators. Overlap with advanced auto/robotics supply chains is real and underappreciated.

Right now most investable exposure is still upstream components or inside large-cap winners like $TSLA and $AMZN (warehouse deployments scaling today).
But the pure-play IPO/exit wave is no longer theoretical:

Tesla is converting Fremont Model S/X lines — Optimus V3 production ramp starts Summer 2026. Public sales targeted toward late 2027. This is the strongest signal yet that 2026 is the bridge year from prototype to early volume.

Unitree (China’s volume leader — already shipping thousands of humanoids/quadrupeds with ~60% gross margins) filed its STAR Market IPO in March targeting ~$7B valuation. First major liquidity event.

Agility Robotics (Digit bots already working with Amazon, GXO, etc.) is going public via SPAC soon (expected ticker $AGLT).

Global IPO season for pure-play humanoids & robotics platforms is kicking off in H2 2026 and runs hot into 2027

text:
@OpenAI’s first custom chip, Jalapeño, built with @Broadcom for inference.

Everyone’s reading it as a shot at @nvidia. 

The deeper signal is that at this scale, the bottleneck isn’t just the compute, it’s the data moving between them.

Copper’s running out and Broadcom’s other big bet is the answer is photonics.

#photonics #copper #ai #openai

text:
Weekly portfolio update:

A rough week for the portfolio finishing
9.8% down 🔴

My photonics names $SIVE & $IQE took big hits this week: $SIVE down 29% & $IQE 7%.

Rolls Royce had a pretty flat week, down 0.14%🔴

All holdings in my pie finished red for the week too.

$VWRP -1.63%🔴
$SEMI -5.31%🔴
$QNTG -3.65%🔴

I crossed £2,000 last Friday, I’m now back under despite depositing £181 this week.

How was your week?👇

text:
The post by the ex-CEO of $SIVE highlights that $SIVE direct share issuance

Which was expanded to 700M SEK due to high institutional demand, implies a solid strategic plan presented during the previous roadshow

As a former CEO, his experience indicates that investors do not commit this growth capital without a clear vision of the company's future in AI photonics, SATCOM, and defense

This reinforces confidence in $SIVE, strengthening its balance sheet and flexibility to accelerate execution in booming sectors

Every heavyweight figure around $SIVE is highly bullish

How could you be bearish just because it dropped 40% after a 2,000% run in a few months? Hahaha

Let $SIVE cook

text:
Serenity is overexposed to optics and photonics, especially in $SIVE and $AAOI

It is worth highlighting that, just like the rest of us, they have been suffering major drops these past few weeks

This goes to show you that even the biggest geniuses lose money at times, since a thesis needs time to play out

Stocks don't just move up in a straight line, and especially with something like $SIVE, after a 2,000% run

It's completely normal for it to take a breather and undergo some corrections

Serenity firmly believe in the timeline for the start of mass production beginning in 2027

Serenity believe it is the most explosive, highest potential, and fastest-growing sector in the entire industry

But that it's still a bit early, and this volatility is the price to pay for being so far ahead of the curve

Very bullish on $SIVE and $AAOI

AI Infrastructure (The Pick & Shovel Layer)Semiconductors: $NVDA $AMD $ARM $INTC AMAT
Memory: MU $SNDK $WDC $STX
Networking: AVGO MRVL CRDO ANET ALAB
Photonics: AAOI LITE COHR NVTS GLW AEHR
Power & Electrification: $VRT $GEV $NNE
Data Centers: $IREN $CIFR $APLD $NBIS
Nuclear: $OKLO $SMR $CCJ
Copper: $FCX $SCCO $TECK

AI Applications & Adjacent Themes
Software: MSFT $NOW $SNOW
Defense: PLTR $KTOS $AVAV
Robotics: OUST $SYM $TSLA
Space: ASTS $RKLB $RDW $LUNR
Quantum: IONQ $QBTS $RGTI
Fintech: HOOD $SOFI $AFRM
Drones: ONDS $DPRO $UMAC
Autonomous: JOBY $ACHR

This framework helps track how capital flows through the entire AI stack. Infrastructure names benefit first from capex, while applications capture monetization later.

text:
A company growing 157% gets punished for slowing down — except Credo's stock tripled instead of cratering. 

$CRDO

The textbook says triple-digit revenue growth decelerating two quarters in a row is a red flag. Short the momentum darling, rotation's coming, the story is cracking.

Credo went the other direction. Up 190% in three months while growth rates dropped from the 200s to the 100s. The forward P/E stretched from 29 to 43, and analysts still call it cheap.

Either the market's lost its mind or the deceleration narrative here is completely backwards.

Here's what's actually happening. Credo isn't slowing because demand rolled over. It's slowing because the supply chain can't keep up. CEO said it plainly on the call — significant tightness across their suppliers. They're selling everything they can get their hands on and leaving orders on the table.

That's not a demand problem. That's a good problem. There's a massive difference between "customers stopped buying" and "we physically can't make enough." The former kills margins as you fight for scraps. The latter — gross margin at Credo just hit 68.3%, up 90 basis points year over year.

When you're capacity constrained and gross margin is expanding, you have pricing power. The market's paying you more per unit because they need what you've got and there aren't enough alternatives. Credo's not discounting to chase volume. They're allocating scarce supply to the highest bidders.

Operating margin tells the same story. It jumped from 36.8% to 49.6% in the fourth quarter. Revenue grew 157% but operating expenses grew way slower. R&D spending led the increase, which means they're plowing money into the next generation of product, not desperately trying to salvage the current cycle with sales and marketing blitzes.

The guidance for Q1 is another triple-digit print — 111% at the midpoint. Still decelerating in percentage terms, still faster than almost anything else you'll find. Gross margin guidance sits right in line with last year, maybe a bit better. Operating expense growth at 61% trails revenue growth by 50 points, so operating leverage keeps improving.

None of this looks like a company hitting a wall. It looks like a company being throttled by how fast the backend can ship components.

The optical push is the other half that the market's pricing in. Hyperscalers are running into the physical limits of copper interconnects at these data rates. 800G, 1.6T, 3.2T — you need optics or you're not scaling the next wave of AI training and inference clusters. Credo's been moving aggressively into this with their own development and they just closed the DustPhotonics acquisition in late May.

DustPhotonics brings silicon photonics technology for near-packaged and co-packaged optics. Credo's now positioned across the whole optical stack for the speeds that matter in the next 18 to 36 months. They're guiding for over $600 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. That's not a side bet — it's becoming a core growth driver.

SpaceX getting into AI cloud is another tailwind. They've already signed Anthropic and Alphabet as customers. If they're building out infrastructure at the scale everyone expects, Credo's interconnects are on the buyer list. Supply constraints or not, the addressable market for high-speed connectivity is expanding faster than almost anyone modeled a year ago.

The risk that spooked people today is Meta saying they might sell excess AI compute capacity. The bears immediately jumped on it as proof of overbuilding. I'm not buying the panic. Meta spent aggressively and may have overshot their own internal needs. That doesn't mean there's market-wide overcapacity or that enterprise AI demand is softening. It means Meta has spare capacity and sees a commercial opportunity. If anything, it validates that the infrastructure they built works and that external demand exists.

Watch it, but it's not a structural red flag for someone like Credo. If real demand were collapsing, you'd see it in their backlog and guidance. You'd see price pressure and margin compression. You're seeing the opposite.

The valuation argument is straightforward. Forward P/E of 43 sounds rich in a vacuum. Compared to a company growing revenue at 111% and earnings even faster with expanding margins, it's not. The forward PEG ratio sits at 0.98, which is below 1.0 and nearly 30% cheaper than the broader IT sector. The market's giving you triple-digit growth at a discount to the sector multiple when you adjust for that growth.

If the supply constraints ease and Credo can actually deliver the revenue they're leaving on the table, earnings estimates move up and the P/E compresses from here. If the optical ramp plays out and they take real share in that multi-hundred-million-dollar opportunity, the growth duration extends and the multiple has room to expand, not contract.

The risk is if something breaks in AI infrastructure spending or if competition in optical heats up faster than expected and margins crack. Right now neither is showing up in the numbers. Demand is strong, supply is the limit, pricing holds, and they're investing in the right areas.

Credo tripled because the growth is real, the margin story is clean, and the positioning for the next leg — optical connectivity at hyperscale — is stronger than it was six months ago. The deceleration everyone's worried about is a feature, not a bug. It's what happens when you sell out of everything you can build.

The stock's not expensive. It's just no longer obscenely cheap. That's what happens when the market figures out the story before you do.

Short the momentum darling, rotation's coming, the story is cracking. Credo went the other direction. Up 190% in three months while growth rates dropped from the 200s to the 100s. The forward P/E stretched from 29 to 43, and analysts still call it cheap. Either the market's lost its mind or the deceleration narrative here is completely backwards. Here's what's actually happening. Credo isn't slowing because demand rolled over. It's slowing because the supply chain can't keep up. CEO said it plainly on the call — significant tightness across their suppliers. They're selling everything they can get their hands on and leaving orders on the table. That's not a demand problem. That's a good problem. There's a massive difference between "customers stopped buying" and "we physically can't make enough." The former kills margins as you fight for scraps. The latter — gross margin at Credo just hit 68.3%, up 90 basis points year over year. When you're capacity constrained and gross margin is expanding, you have pricing power. The market's paying you more per unit because they need what you've got and there aren't enough alternatives. Credo's not discounting to chase volume. They're allocating scarce supply to the highest bidders. Operating margin tells the same story. It jumped from 36.8% to 49.6% in the fourth quarter. Revenue grew 157% but operating expenses grew way slower. R&D spending led the increase, which means they're plowing money into the next generation of product, not desperately trying to salvage the current cycle with sales and marketing blitzes. The guidance for Q1 is another triple-digit print — 111% at the midpoint. Still decelerating in percentage terms, still faster than almost anything else you'll find. Gross margin guidance sits right in line with last year, maybe a bit better. Operating expense growth at 61% trails revenue growth by 50 points, so operating leverage keeps improving. None of this looks like a company hitting a wall. It looks like a company being throttled by how fast the backend can ship components. The optical push is the other half that the market's pricing in. Hyperscalers are running into the physical limits of copper interconnects at these data rates. 800G, 1.6T, 3.2T — you need optics or you're not scaling the next wave of AI training and inference clusters. Credo's been moving aggressively into this with their own development and they just closed the DustPhotonics acquisition in late May. DustPhotonics brings silicon photonics technology for near-packaged and co-packaged optics. Credo's now positioned across the whole optical stack for the speeds that matter in the next 18 to 36 months. They're guiding for over $600 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. That's not a side bet — it's becoming a core growth driver. SpaceX getting into AI cloud is another tailwind. They've already signed Anthropic and Alphabet as customers. If they're building out infrastructure at the scale everyone expects, Credo's interconnects are on the buyer list. Supply constraints or not, the addressable market for high-speed connectivity is expanding faster than almost anyone modeled a year ago. The risk that spooked people today is Meta saying they might sell excess AI compute capacity. The bears immediately jumped on it as proof of overbuilding. I'm not buying the panic. Meta spent aggressively and may have overshot their own internal needs. That doesn't mean there's market-wide overcapacity or that enterprise AI demand is softening. It means Meta has spare capacity and sees a commercial opportunity. If anything, it validates that the infrastructure they built works and that external demand exists. Watch it, but it's not a structural red flag for someone like Credo. If real demand were collapsing, you'd see it in their backlog and guidance. You'd see price pressure and margin compression. You're seeing the opposite. The valuation argument is straightforward. Forward P/E of 43 sounds rich in a vacuum. Compared to a company growing revenue at 111% and earnings even faster with expanding margins, it's not. The forward PEG ratio sits at 0.98, which is below 1.0 and nearly 30% cheaper than the broader IT sector. The market's giving you triple-digit growth at a discount to the sector multiple when you adjust for that growth. If the supply constraints ease and Credo can actually deliver the revenue they're leaving on the table, earnings estimates move up and the P/E compresses from here. If the optical ramp plays out and they take real share in that multi-hundred-million-dollar opportunity, the growth duration extends and the multiple has room to expand, not contract. The risk is if something breaks in AI infrastructure spending or if competition in optical heats up faster than expected and margins crack. Right now neither is showing up in the numbers. Demand is strong, supply is the limit, pricing holds, and they're investing in the right areas. Credo tripled because the growth is real, the margin story is clean, and the positioning for the next leg — optical connectivity at hyperscale — is stronger than it was six months ago. The deceleration everyone's worried about is a feature, not a bug. It's what happens when you sell out of everything you can build. The stock's not expensive. It's just no longer obscenely cheap. That's what happens when the market figures out the story before you do. text:
Market Review 20260701
- Rotation
- Money is leaving: AI trades, ie semiconductors, photonics, memory, data centers, neoclouds, space names etc
- Money is entering: Biotech, healthcare, fintech, finance, cybersecurity, selected software
- The rotation has not changed much. I was cautiously optimistic that the market could do well from here but looks like the recent leaders and the broad market are not ready yet. 
- Megacaps like the MAG7 caught a bit of a bid but I will want to see more life returning, and hope this is not just a dead cat bounce for them. 
- The big news today would be $META saying they want to sell their excess compute capacity and create an AI Cloud business. I don't think that is so easy. META is mainly an advertising / communications company. Building an AI cloud is not just about repositioning chips. That triggered broad selling across the AI plays, especially the neoclouds as that is what they had pivoted to doing from bitcoin mining. This is the "EXCUSE" that the market is looking for to sell. It was already wide and loose as I have mentioned in several posts recently. 
- $MU breaking its 20SMA and going back below 1000 will not be a good look. Photonics had already lost their leadership a few weeks ago. If Memory also loose leadership then the entire AI play may have to take a longer pause. Semiconductors are kindda holding it but also on the verge of loosing it. 
- I have also noticed some of the "old dogs" coming back to life. These are mainly software and fintech. Talking about HOOD, SOFI, UPST, DUOL, LMND, FIGR etc. Will they really resurrect and start leading?
- All the best.

text:
Hypothetically if hyperscalers paused for too long others would eventually step in. But there’s little incentive for an extended pause. They’d only be motivated to potentially ease off in the near term without fully stepping away. I’m saying this in the context of their stocks facing pressure, but more importantly how much future demand is being priced into may infra plays (photonics, semicaps, etc.). Forward PEs look very expensive if growth slows even modestly.

That said, I’m less convinced others could meaningfully fill any real HS gap if a true air pocket materializes in 27/28. Sovereigns tend to copycat hyperscaler/frontier thinking rather than lead aggressively on their own. China will likely stay aggressive, but their more efficient models mean they won’t drive as much incremental compute demand on a relative basis. I wouldn’t count on neoclouds ramping up enough given their heavy debt loads from this cycle, and other private infra players are generally conservative by nature and won’t swing big to backfill. So while some substitution happens, I doubt they’ll be as aggressive as the HS have been.

This would open the door to a potential air pocket where caution starts outweighing fresh spend and proven demand. Again, this is just an asymmetric optic that isn’t priced in at all after this latest bull run - a possible near-term correction where the “indefinite demand > supply” narrative gets tested.

This is all speculative and is probably too early. The HS rhetoric has been nothing but aggressive so far. But any rally/relief valve in their stocks might be welcomed as a signal that they recognize the FCF strain and aren’t being reckless.

They’ve been able to afford this level of spending so far thanks to their balance sheets, and it has also helped motivate vendors to keep pushing supply. But for how much longer? Especially if they start needing to lever with debt before the next real demand wave hits?

The main missing piece of compute capacity right now seems tied to big agentic/agent workflows at enterprise scale. But even that might arrive slower than hoped (due to internal politics, red tape, other integration hurdles, learning curve). A lot of today’s visible demand is also hype driven/token maxxing - whether its enterprises testing things out or retail usage that may not translate into sustained high value workloads.

I do feel that memory isn’t in the same bucket as broader infra on a valuation basis. It’s hard to fathom we’re still in such good shape valuation-wise after this run. One thing I forgot to mention in my post is that it’s not only the multimodal needs and emerging use cases outpacing new efficiencies (around KV cache, quantization, etc.), but also the deeper architectural reality of the memory stack itself. Model weights/parameters and activations/intermediate tensors (and optimizer states/gradients for any training or fine-tuning) are often larger than KV cache needs in most workloads. And these layers can’t all be tackled with the same KV-specific compression or efficiency tricks.

text:
Inside Kopin's Vision for the Future.

What role will optical interconnects, MicroLED technology, and U.S.-based manufacturing play in the next generation of technology and AI?

Kopin CEO, President, and Chairperson Michael Murray sat down with Martin Gagel of @RadiusResearch to discuss Kopin's strategy, the evolution of jointly developed Neural I/o™, and the opportunities emerging at the intersection of AI infrastructure and advanced photonics.

For $SIVE, migrating to NASDAQ isn't just about liquidity; it’s about shifting the valuation to institutional investors who actually understand upstream photonics architecture and 60% GM profiles.

text:
Bears most of the time actually don't misunderstand. 

They reframe it to fill their egos on why they are on calling out stocks like $SIVE and $AAOI not being good buys today.

It is so funny how most of the market goes against these names when things are down due to macro conditions, with nothing related actually to the fundamentals/thesis of such companies. 

InP laser shortage is going to kick in next year. 1.6T/CPO timelines will keep compressing.

Obviously the gross margins and revenue growth will show on the balance sheet of these photonic companies. Afterall, it is the next bull cycle of the overall AI buildout.

Everyone betted against $MU, $SNDK last year. Now they are the so called bulls that called it early. 2026 is showing memory shortage.

A lot of investments & money is going into delivering when ramps start to scale in 2027. This was confirmed by earnings from $LITE, $COHR, $AAOI, and many others. 

BoFA and GS reports back this well. They aren't your cheap X account researches that throw out rubbish. These are institutions with a big ecosystem connection to the overall technological industry, including AI companies. Analysts, experts and technicians in the field write these reports that take weeks (maybe months), not just a few days of research. 

After all being said and research by @aleabitoreddit and all others, if one still doesn't believe in the story playing out and why this is true, I think it would be good to reconsider and have a check in the mirror if you are just a soft hand in the market with absolutely zero real conviction wherever you invest. Today it could be photonics. Tomorrow you'll find another one. 

Some habits don't change. It requires a change in the overall mindset and character of the investor. That is why I always reinforce the need to construct your own system, and not blindly follow anybody. Even me. 

- AI

text:
Who is Coherent ( $COHR )? 

Coherent is a leader in photonics and laser technology, supplying high-speed optical components used in AI data centers and advanced networking.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang recently broke ground on Coherent’s new U.S. laser factory, showing that NVIDIA is securing optics supply early the same strategy it used with HBM and advanced packaging.

Financials & Outlook
• Datacom segment showing solid growth driven by AI demand
• Focus on margin expansion while building next-gen photonics capacity
• AI optics viewed as a key multi-year growth driver into 2026–2027

What Separates Coherent
• Broader portfolio than pure transceiver plays like $AAOI 
• Deeper vertical integration (laser chips to modules) vs. $LITE
• Direct $NVDA partnership on domestic manufacturing adds strategic weight

The recent pullback has created a small entry point on a name with growing importance in the AI supply chain. 

Let me know what you guys think about this one has it ran its course or is it still a buy ?

I'm actually more bullish than ever as prices go down. $SIVE at ~$1.9B MC you have: - $GFS reference laser - laser for Ayar and others in $NVDA NVLink ecosystem for CPO scale up - $POET and others for CPO scale out - $JBL and others for 1.6T+ pluggable optical transcivers - O-Net for ELS mass production So you have all these hyperscaler suppliers trying to create as much as possible with Sivers as the laser bottleneck... And it's just a matter of how much Win Semi + others partners can make, with $SIVE receiving ~60% gross margins + optionality to TAM expand downward. The 15% recent share expansion that Swedish media have been bearposting is for NASDAQ listing float + M&A (and it's authorization). You just have potential short sellers running illegal bot farms, Swedish media bearposting a company Swedish exchange, on top of a general macro drop. The ~$140M convertible note is real, but it's pennies to US institutions. We'll likely see more institutional ownership when data comes out. But this is also why it's good for $SIVE to prioritize NASDAQ listing so they don't need to deal with this noise and local media. With $AAOI at $10B, you have: - A end-to-end US laser/design/assembly player projecting $471m month revenue H1 entering H2 2027. - With other hyperscalers like $AMD apparently discussing LTAs. And they probably are sitting on a ton of cash after running ~$1.4B in ATMs (400M + 400m+ 600m). Kinda all that needs to be said with those insane revenue projections as long as management doesn't BS. This just reminds me of when $NBIS crashed to $70 last year even while projecting $7-9B ARR, and as timelines got closer it recovered to $250+. Feels like dejavu. I'm just waiting for both volume ramps to hit. text:
Millimeter-wave (mmWave) technology is powering the next generation of ultra-fast wireless communication. Discover innovative antennas, electro-optic modulators, and key components driving 5G and beyond. 

https://t.co/TIc1uCneZN

#5G #mmWave #Photonics #Wireless #Telecom https://t.co/pZV8Uzeg9y

$IQE
One of the most important points from FY2025, in my view, was that IQE is seeing increasing customer demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers, while its LWIR product has achieved production qualification with a Tier 1 customer.

I believe infrared-based LWIR/MWIR, and potentially SWIR as well, could become a very important growth pillar for IQE through 2030.

IQE’s sensing portfolio can broadly be divided into four platforms:

GaAs: VCSEL / 3D sensing
InP: SWIR / photonics
GaN: LiDAR drivers / microLED
GaSb: IR detectors / T2SL / MWIR / LWIR

In other words, IQE is a rare pure-play merchant epitaxy foundry that owns all the key III-V platforms: GaAs, InP, GaN, and GaSb.

Among these, the area I am particularly focused on is GaSb-based MWIR/LWIR.

Robotics, drones, autonomous driving, and defence systems are still in the early stages of growth.

Today, many systems combine RGB cameras, LiDAR, radar, ToF, and other sensors, but as these systems become more advanced, sensing will not end with RGB cameras alone.

RGB cameras are extremely powerful in the visible spectrum.

They are excellent for recognizing colour, shape, text, and surface-level information.

However, visible-light-based data has clear limitations.

At night, in smoke, dust, fog, strong backlight, camouflage, and adverse weather, RGB cameras alone cannot provide sufficiently robust information.

Future robots, drones, and unmanned systems will not simply need to “see” what is visible.

They will also need to understand the thermal information emitted by people, vehicles, engines, batteries, and industrial equipment.

This is where MWIR/LWIR becomes increasingly important.

MWIR is linked to high-temperature objects, engines, missiles, long-range surveillance, aerospace, and defence systems.

LWIR is linked to people, animals, machine heat, night surveillance, thermal imaging, and hazardous-environment perception.

In other words, MWIR/LWIR may not merely be a “supporting sensor” for future autonomous systems.

In certain environments, it could become a core perception layer that is even more important than RGB.

This is especially true in defence.

The future battlefield is moving toward drones, unmanned vehicles, surveillance systems, automatic target recognition, and night operations.

In these environments, high-resolution RGB images alone are not enough.

To detect people in darkness, vehicles with residual engine heat, hidden equipment, missile-launch heat signatures, or abnormal machine heat, MWIR/LWIR thermal sensing is required.

The same logic applies to robotics.

If humanoids or industrial robots only operate in bright indoor environments, RGB + depth sensors may be enough.

But once they expand into dark factories, warehouses, night patrol, disaster sites, military robotics, and machine overheating detection, the story changes.

For robots, MWIR/LWIR can become both “eyes that see at night” and a “sense that detects heat.”

The problem is that existing MWIR/LWIR sensors have been very expensive.

High-performance IR detectors have been costly because of small substrates, complex epitaxy, low yields, ROIC hybrid bonding, cryocoolers, and expensive optics.

As a result, they have mainly been used in markets that can absorb the cost, such as defence, aerospace, and specialised industrial applications.

But IQE’s 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafer directly targets one of these bottlenecks.

When wafer size increases, it is not simply about making the wafer bigger.

The number of dies per wafer increases, large-area manufacturing productivity improves, and there is room for better uniformity and yield.

This creates the foundation for producing high resolution FPAs focal plane arrays more economically.

Simply put, high performance thermal sensing, which was previously too expensive and limited mainly to defence and specialised applications, may have the potential to move into broader markets through large diameter epi platforms.

IQE is not talking about this as a simple R&D project.

It has mentioned both Tier 1 production qualification for its LWIR product and increasing demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers.

As humanoid physical AI models mature and need more sensing vectors to become robust against real-world noise, I believe there will come a point where additional modalities become necessary.

In autonomous driving, this could happen even earlier, with MWIR/LWIR potentially being integrated sooner.

The fact that production qualification has already been completed with a Tier 1 customer suggests that this demand is not theoretical anymore. It may already be starting.

Beyond data centres, how do you view IQE’s future in robotics and autonomous driving?

As humanoid physical AI models mature and need more sensing vectors to become robust against real-world noise, I believe there will come a point where additional modalities become necessary. In autonomous driving, this could happen even earlier, with MWIR/LWIR potentially being integrated sooner. The fact that production qualification has already been completed with a Tier 1 customer suggests that this demand is not theoretical anymore. It may already be starting. Beyond data centres, how do you view IQE’s future in robotics and autonomous driving?"}}}, "rest_id": "2071277131095818361", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1853", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Die Verlagerung der Halbleitersicherungsqualität von Siliziumkarbid hin zur optischen KI Infrastruktur

Der explosive Anstieg der Rechenlasten in modernen Rechenzentren verändert die Anforderungen an die physische Zuverlässigkeit von Mikrochips grundlegend. Da der Ausfall einzelner Komponenten in massiven KI Clustern immense finanzielle Schäden verursacht, rücken spezialisierte Verfahren zur Qualitätsprüfung direkt auf Wafer-Ebene ins Zentrum der globalen Wertschöpfungskette.
Das US Halbleiterprüfunternehmen Aehr Test Systems demonstriert diese transformationale Dynamik in der ersten Hälfte des Jahres 2026 durch einen beispiellosen Kursanstieg an den Finanzmärkten. Die Aktie, die zu Beginn des Jahres noch nahe der 20 US Dollar Marke notierte, kletterte im Juni 2026 zeitweise auf über 115 US Dollar. Dieser Zuwachs spiegelt den erfolgreichen strategischen Wandel des Unternehmens wider. Während Aehr Test Systems in den vergangenen Jahren primär von Systemen für Siliziumkarbid-Leistungshalbleiter im stagnierenden Elektromobilitätssektor abhing, positioniert sich der Konzern nun als kritischer Infrastrukturpartner für die optische Datenübertragung in KI Rechenzentren.
Den technologischen Hebel bildet die proprietäre FOX-XP Plattform, ein vollautomatisiertes System für das sogenannte Wafer-Level Burn-In. Bei diesem Verfahren werden hunderte Halbleiter-Wafer gleichzeitig unter extremen thermischen und elektrischen Bedingungen künstlich gealtert, um fehlerhafte Bauteile noch vor dem finalen Verpackungsprozess zu identifizieren. Ein entscheidender Engpass moderner Beschleuniger-Cluster liegt in der Kommunikation über optische Transceiver (Silicon Photonics), die Lichtsignale statt elektrischer Impulse nutzen. Da diese optischen Schnittstellen extrem anfällig für frühe Ausfälle sind, verlangen führende Hyperscaler eine lückenlose Vorabprüfung.
Die finanzielle Bestätigung dieser Neuausrichtung lieferten die jüngsten Auftragseingänge. Nach einem vorangegangenen, operativ schwächeren Quartal mit lediglich 10,3 Millionen US Dollar Umsatz meldete Aehr für die zweite Jahreshälfte 2026 einen massiven Sprung bei den Buchungen auf über 92 Millionen US Dollar. Treiber war eine Serie von Großaufträgen, darunter eine Rekordbestellung über 41 Millionen US Dollar von einem führenden Hyperscale-KI Kunden sowie Folgeaufträge eines globalen Marktführers für Netzwerklösungen. Diese Systeme dienen sowohl der technologischen Qualifikation neuer Chip-Designs als auch der anschließenden Hochvolumenproduktion.
Trotz dieser operativen Meilensteine bleibt die Aktie an den Kapitalmärkten von hoher Volatilität geprägt, was sich in einer temporären Korrektur Ende Juni auf rund 91 US Dollar äußerte. Mit einem Kurs-Umsatz-Verhältnis von über 60 reflektiert die Bewertung bereits erhebliche Vorschusslorbeeren auf die kommenden Fiskaljahre. Da das Unternehmen den kapitalintensiven Hochlauf zudem über ein aktives Aktienplatzierungsprogramm (At-The-Market) mitfinanziert, müssen die anstehenden Quartalszahlen eine fehlerfreie Skalierung der Auslieferungen belegen. Für den Technologiesektor untermauert der Fall Aehr jedoch ein fundamentales Prinzip: Der wirtschaftliche Erfolg im KI Zyklus verschiebt sich zunehmend von den reinen Chip-Designern hin zu den hochspezialisierten Enablern, welche die physische Betriebssicherheit des Gesamtsystems garantieren.

text:
$IONQ is looking to become vertically integrated.

The acquisition of $SKYT seems like the missing piece.

It’s not.

$IONQ buys laser systems from Coherent and NKT Photonics.

Coherent is a US company that supplies lasers to $IONQ called Coherent Paladin.

The ultraviolet lasers produce light at specific wavelengths for the atomic transitions in barium and ytterbium that $IONQ uses.

Those are standard benchtop setups for general qubit manipulation. They’re massive and require lab environment to operate.

Then there’s NKT Photonics from Denmark. It supplies tailor made crystal fiber lasers with low noise. The goal here is to use the lasers in datacenters.

The setup is smaller so that the lasers can be mounted in a 19-inch rack with the other components of the quantum computer.

The lasers are ultra-stable and narrow-linewidth. It’s challenging to simply swap them with anyone else’s lasers.

Which makes $IONQ vulnerable to this specific supply chain.

Coherent relies on gallium, germanium, and rare-earth elements, most of which are mined in China. China suspended its total ban on exporting critical dual-use metals to the US until November 27, 2026.

What will happen after? Nobody knows.

We know from Coherent’s 2023 update that it had ample inventories and suppliers outside of China.

NKT Photonics sources nonlinear crystals used to build frequency converters from Europe, Japan, and the US.

The dangers here are regulations and delays.

NKT Photonics is a subsidiary of Hamamatsu Photonics. It has preferential access to Hamamatsu’s supply chain of laser diodes and optical components.

Trade wars between Europe and Japan are unlikely, but if they happen, NKT Photonics will be affected.

Finally, its lasers are dual use: They can be used in quantum computers and military guidance or radar systems.

Any sudden restrictions by Danish authorities could result in bottlenecks.

$IONQ is managing its supply chain nicely but dangers remain.

The acquisition of $SKYT seems like the missing piece. It’s not. $IONQ buys laser systems from Coherent and NKT Photonics. Coherent is a US company that supplies lasers to $IONQ called Coherent Paladin. The ultraviolet lasers produce light at specific wavelengths for the atomic transitions in barium and ytterbium that $IONQ uses. Those are standard benchtop setups for general qubit manipulation. They’re massive and require lab environment to operate. Then there’s NKT Photonics from Denmark. It supplies tailor made crystal fiber lasers with low noise. The goal here is to use the lasers in datacenters. The setup is smaller so that the lasers can be mounted in a 19-inch rack with the other components of the quantum computer. The lasers are ultra-stable and narrow-linewidth. It’s challenging to simply swap them with anyone else’s lasers. Which makes $IONQ vulnerable to this specific supply chain. Coherent relies on gallium, germanium, and rare-earth elements, most of which are mined in China. China suspended its total ban on exporting critical dual-use metals to the US until November 27, 2026. What will happen after? Nobody knows. We know from Coherent’s 2023 update that it had ample inventories and suppliers outside of China. NKT Photonics sources nonlinear crystals used to build frequency converters from Europe, Japan, and the US. The dangers here are regulations and delays. NKT Photonics is a subsidiary of Hamamatsu Photonics. It has preferential access to Hamamatsu’s supply chain of laser diodes and optical components. Trade wars between Europe and Japan are unlikely, but if they happen, NKT Photonics will be affected. Finally, its lasers are dual use: They can be used in quantum computers and military guidance or radar systems. Any sudden restrictions by Danish authorities could result in bottlenecks. $IONQ is managing its supply chain nicely but dangers remain."}}}, "rest_id": "2072033679514468359", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4535", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}
