text:
Thread

Everyone keeps asking the same question.

"Which company wins AI?" 
$NVDA $AVGO $AMD $TSM $MU $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META $ORCL $PLTR ?

I think that's the wrong question.

The better question is:

Which company becomes impossible to replace?

The AI infrastructure buildout isn't being constrained by GPUs anymore.

It's becoming constrained by everything around them.

Power.

Cooling.

Memory.

Networking.

And increasingly...

Light.

Most investors still think optical networking is about transceivers.

It isn't.

The real value is quietly moving upstream.

Every new optical architecture—whether it's pluggables, Scale-Out CPO, Scale-Up CPO, Near-Packaged Optics, or External Laser Source (ELS)—still depends on one thing:

A stable, high-volume CW laser.

Different architectures.

Same dependency.

That's where supply-chain mapping becomes interesting.

This is why I continue spending far more time studying suppliers than hyperscalers.

Hyperscalers change vendors.

Platforms evolve.

Chip architectures change.

But infrastructure bottlenecks?

Those usually last much longer than people expect.

The companies solving those bottlenecks often capture the highest operating leverage of the entire cycle.

This is exactly why I find $SIVE so interesting.

Most people still describe Sivers as "a laser company."

I think that's missing the point.

The product isn't the thesis.

Its position in the supply chain is.

If multiple AI networking architectures all require the same laser technology, then the value isn't coming from any single customer.

It's coming from becoming embedded across the ecosystem.

That's a completely different investment framework.

People also underestimate how infrastructure cycles actually work.

Revenue is always the last thing to arrive.

First comes R&D.

Then qualification.

Then design wins.

Then ecosystem validation.

Only after all of that do production ramps begin.

Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity.

One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing.

Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter.

I don't.

The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027.

That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines.

I've said this before:

I don't invest by asking,

"Who sells the most AI chips?"

I ask,

"Who does everyone else quietly depend on?"

Those companies rarely make headlines.

But they often become the biggest winners of the cycle.

That's why I continue mapping bottlenecks instead of narratives.

NFA. DYODD.@aleabitoreddit #StockMarket  #Investing #AI

Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity. One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing. Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter. I don't. The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027. That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines. I've said this before: I don't invest by asking, "Who sells the most AI chips?" I ask, "Who does everyone else quietly depend on?" Those companies rarely make headlines. But they often become the biggest winners of the cycle. That's why I continue mapping bottlenecks instead of narratives. NFA. text:
💰 Nyemissioner - positivt eller negativt? Allt handlar om syftet.

Jag har märkt att många småsparare och nya investerare automatiskt kopplar ordet nyemission till något negativt och därför blir oroliga.

Det är fullt förståeligt – många nyemissioner genomförs när bolag har ekonomiska problem.

Men det betyder inte att alla nyemissioner ser likadana ut. I detta fallet handlar det dessutom om en riktad nyemission som riktas direkt mot större institutionella investerare och kommer dessutom genomföras över natten.

⸻

I Sivers, $SIVE, fall är budskapet från bolaget tydligt:

Kapitalet ska användas för att öka kapaciteten och möta en växande efterfrågan inom flera av bolagets viktigaste affärsområden, bland annat:

✅ Optiska lasrar och optiska förstärkare för AI-datacenter

✅ Automotive LiDAR

✅ Fortsatt expansion inom Photonics

Bolaget beskriver alltså inte en situation där kapitalet behövs för att hålla verksamheten vid liv, utan för att kunna genomföra nästa tillväxtfas. Det är en viktig skillnad!

⸻

Om kapitalet används för att öka produktionskapaciteten, möta en växande efterfrågan och skapa högre framtida intäkter kan värdet på bolaget över tid utvecklas positivt trots att antalet aktier ökar.

Det är just därför syftet med en nyemission är minst lika viktigt som nyemissionen i sig.

⸻

Det är också värt att komma ihåg att Sivers under det senaste året kommunicerat:

🔹 En växande pipeline inom AI-datacenter.

🔹 Produktionsupptrappning inom Automotive LiDAR från H2 2026.

🔹 Flera planerade produktionsramper under 2026–2027.

Det är mot den bakgrunden jag tycker att den här nyemissionen ska bedömas.

Personligen ser jag denna nyemissionen har ett väldigt starkt signalvärde kring vart bolaget är på väg!

⸻

Detta är inte en köp- eller säljrekommendation.

Jag tycker bara att det är viktigt att läsa hela pressmeddelandet och förstå varför bolaget tar in kapital – inte bara reagera på ordet nyemission!

text:
$SIVE is being mispriced because the market is still reading it as a small photonics hardware vendor.

That framing is outdated.

The real position is inside the AI optical stack, at a specific constraint layer: the laser source.

AI networking stack is shifting fast:

GPU clusters
→ scale-out bandwidth demand
→ 800G to 1.6T optics transition
→ CPO / LPO architecture shift
→ laser source layer becomes the constraint
→ InP / EML / CW laser ecosystem

Sivers sits in that lower layer.

Not as a module assembler.

But closer to the optical source and integration bottleneck that determines whether higher-level optics can actually scale.

The key shift the market is missing:

AI is no longer primarily a compute scaling problem.

It is becoming a data movement problem.

And data movement is limited by optical physics, not silicon performance.

Three structural points:

First, bandwidth demand in AI clusters is accelerating faster than electrical interconnect scaling.

Second, 1.6T and CPO architectures increase dependency on stable, high-quality laser sources.

Third, once optical components are designed into a platform, replacement cycles are long and sticky.

This changes how SIVE should be viewed.

Not as a cyclical supplier tied to component demand.

But as a potential design-in layer in optical reference architectures.

The bull case is not near-term revenue growth.

It is architectural adoption.

If Sivers becomes embedded in next-generation optical designs, demand becomes structural across hyperscaler buildouts.

Risks remain significant:

early-stage business model
historical dilution
customer concentration
execution risk in scaling photonics manufacturing

This is still a high-volatility setup.

But the key re-rating driver is simple:

Control points in AI systems are shifting from compute to connectivity, and from connectivity to optical physics.

That is where Sivers sits.@aleabitoreddit 
#StockMarket  #Investing

text:
$ALMU $ALMU continues building the team needed to move from R&D to commercial production.

The company appointed Brendan Moran, Ph.D. as Vice President of Engineering, a key hire as Aeluma prepares to scale manufacturing of its compound semiconductor technology.

Moran brings 18 years at Lumileds, where he led product strategy and development for a mobile flash business generating over $300 million in annual revenue. He also oversaw the launch of 25+ products into high-volume manufacturing, producing more than 4 billion semiconductor devices, and managed engineering teams responsible for taking products from development to mass production.

Why this matters:

Developing breakthrough semiconductor technology is only half the challenge. The harder part is turning lab-scale innovation into reliable, high-volume manufacturing that customers can deploy. That’s exactly where Moran’s experience fits.

For Aeluma, the focus is now shifting from proving its technology to commercializing it across AI infrastructure, optical communications, defense, aerospace, quantum computing, AR/VR, automotive and advanced sensing.

Aeluma’s platform integrates high-performance compound semiconductors with standard silicon manufacturing, aiming to deliver the performance advantages of III-V materials while using scalable semiconductor production processes. If successful, this could lower manufacturing costs while enabling broader adoption in fast-growing AI and photonics markets.

For investors, this is more than a management hire. It’s another signal that Aeluma is preparing for its next phase—moving beyond R&D and positioning itself for commercial revenue, manufacturing scale, and customer deployment.

text:
The physical AI trade feels intuitive because the demand narrative is so visible, but structurally it’s much harder than photonics or memory to anchor on a true bottleneck. 

In memory/optics you can point to very specific capacity and process constraints; with many of these AI hardware names, the value chain is still fluid and the eventual mass‑production suppliers aren’t locked in yet.

I think the better approach is to treat it as an ecosystem that will evolve toward a diversified supplier base, rather than trying to guess the one or two permanent choke points this early. 

The more interesting edge may end up being in companies whose economics improve as the chain diversifies, instead of in whoever looks like “the bottleneck” today.

text:
$16.5 BILLION in robotics VC in one quarter. Nearly 500 deals. All-time high.

This is the capital signal that Physical AI (humanoid robots that see, feel, move, and reason in the real world) is accelerating from prototype to production.

The same infrastructure stack powering today’s AI data center boom, memory chips, photonics, sensing hardware, and edge compute is the same foundation these robots will run on.

Most people are watching the robot makers. Smart money is watching what goes inside every one of them.

The Memory Supercycle No One’s Talking About Yet

• Memory demand is about to get a powerful second leg that compounds through the 2030s
• AI data centers are already strained on HBM and high-performance DRAM supply
• Physical AI adds massive new volume from edge inference, sensor fusion, and real-time control at scale
• One advanced humanoid can consume memory bandwidth and capacity comparable to several high-end AI servers today

Why Robots Need So Much Memory

• Real-time on-device AI inference (no latency for cloud round-trips in dynamic environments)
• Heavy sensor fusion across cameras, LiDAR, tactile/force sensors, IMUs, and proprioception
• Complex whole-body control, balance, dexterous hands, mapping, planning, and task memory
• Training + simulation workloads in data centers also scale as robot fleets generate petabytes of real-world data

Micron’s CEO said it plainly: humanoid robots carry roughly 10x the memory of an average L2+ autonomous vehicle, and he sees a sustained, substantial multi-decade memory demand cycle beginning in the latter part of this decade.

The Stack Overlap Is Direct
The photonics for high-speed interconnects, advanced packaging, and memory architectures built for AI accelerators transfer almost seamlessly to robotics. Training data centers and edge robots are two sides of the same infrastructure coin.

Big Tech Is Already Building This Internally

• Tesla → Optimus humanoid for internal factories + future external use
• Amazon → Deep into warehouse robotics (Agility bots already deployed) and pushing further into AI-driven physical automation

They may design custom systems in-house, but the advanced memory still comes from the specialists who can deliver at scale.

Winners & Timeline
• Micron ( $MU ), SK Hynix, and Samsung — the big three memory leaders
• H2 2026 into 2027 is when multiple pure-play robotics companies are expected to go public (more visibility and capital)
• Memory capacity build-out is happening now because these companies see combined AI infra + robotics demand as structural, not cyclical

How to Get Exposure (You May Already Own It)

One clean, targeted way: the Roundhill Memory ETF ( $DRAM ) is built specifically for pure memory exposure (HBM, DRAM, NAND) unlike broader semiconductor funds.

Recent top holdings include: 
• Samsung Electronics ~25.8% 
• Micron (MU) ~25.45% 
• SK hynix ~24.56%
Together,  these three leaders make up over 75% of the ETF.

Of course there are nuances to the timeline and risks:

Nuance & Balanced View
•Tech, cost, battery, safety, and regulatory hurdles remain real
•Memory has historically been cyclical, but AI + Physical AI together are making demand more structural and multi-decade
•Supply response is coming (new fabs from all three players), yet advanced memory shortages have already persisted, and new demand legs matter
•Geopolitical concentration in the supply chain adds volatility but also reinforces the moat for leaders who execute

The chart makes the signal impossible to ignore, the Q1 ’26 bar towers over everything before it. That capital is accelerating R&D, pilots, and supply chain prep right now.

Physical AI is next.

The memory supercycle already running from AI data centers just got a powerful second engine, and the same companies winning in AI silicon are best positioned for what comes next in the physical world.

Not financial advice. This is one perspective on a long-term theme.

Recent top holdings include: • Samsung Electronics ~25.8% • Micron (MU) ~25.45% • SK hynix ~24.56% Together, these three leaders make up over 75% of the ETF. Of course there are nuances to the timeline and risks: Nuance & Balanced View •Tech, cost, battery, safety, and regulatory hurdles remain real •Memory has historically been cyclical, but AI + Physical AI together are making demand more structural and multi-decade •Supply response is coming (new fabs from all three players), yet advanced memory shortages have already persisted, and new demand legs matter •Geopolitical concentration in the supply chain adds volatility but also reinforces the moat for leaders who execute The chart makes the signal impossible to ignore, the Q1 ’26 bar towers over everything before it. That capital is accelerating R&D, pilots, and supply chain prep right now. Physical AI is next. The memory supercycle already running from AI data centers just got a powerful second engine, and the same companies winning in AI silicon are best positioned for what comes next in the physical world. Not financial advice. text:
Now that Open AI itself has publicly mentioned $CBRS and its benefits I will reaffirm my comments from one week ago.

Cerebras is literally your next Nvidia/Sandisk on a silver platter.

It is the ONLY company using SRAM on their chips. As we’ve seen with the boom in DRAM prices. But what does this mean in actuality? 

They have this BIG wafers; made in their proprietary hardware, that nest the SRAM right next to what is responsible for inference. 

If Photonics is the answer to bringing fast inference to chips; Cerebras solved this bottleneck long ago by using storage slower ram, but sticking it right next to the GPU itself. Making the speeds faster than even anything Nvidia is currently capable of achieving 

It’s such a breakthrough that Open AI itself vested billions into the business. One of the frontier labs is literally saying “this is so valuable to us we need equity in it, and we need their chips ASAP” 

@andrewdfeldman is the real MaCoy in engineering terms; he’s the nerdiest CEO we’ve seen since Zuckerberg.

The finance heads on Wallstreet are correct in assuming the company is “overvalued” for what it is now. 

But the tech heads in Silicon Valley are seeing it the opposite ; 50B is probably cheap for an innovation this critical to expanding model efficiency 

The new model is so powerful the government told open Ai they can’t even release it yet. This is largely because of its speed and efficiency not just the intelligence level. 

The company responsible for that is growing at 90% yearly revenue. With many other segments in its book growing at 40-70% revenue. Issue is profitability. The better they get at producing these at scale; I believe the offers will come rolling. They’ve already made two deals with Amazon and Coreweave. This will expand and I believe even Anthropic+ Space X will wake up and smell the coffee. 

Thank you for your attention to this matter. Longer Analysis coming soon.

text:
GlobalFoundries and Corning jointly demonstrated an optical interconnect solution that combines a silicon photonics (SiPh) chip with a detachable GLASSBRIDGE™ connector. For the first time, they achieved a fully detachable GLASSBRIDGE™ connector capable of handling up to 280 mW of optical power.

The optical interface integrates GF’s silicon photonics platform, featuring SiN spot-size converters (SSCs) and a V-groove edge-coupling structure, with Corning’s GLASSBRIDGE™ connector technology, which utilizes low-loss ion-exchanged glass waveguides. The GF PIC expands the optical mode to match the 9 µm mode field diameter of the glass waveguide, while Corning’s waveguide routes the light to a connector interface compatible with standard MT ferrule connectors.

Just pasting my notes. I am waiting for the better entry / laser diodes (EMLs and VCSELs) that inside optical transceivers. After a massive inventory correction in legacy telecom, their AI optics business has completely bottomed out and is inflecting hard, making them a premier "growth at a reasonable price" asset in AI photonics. 

Lumentum announced major plans to expand its U.S. indium phosphide capacity using new systems. More importantly, the stock is surging following news that it secured a multibillion-dollar supply agreement and a $2 billion strategic investment from Nvidia. Because AI data centers require immense energy, hyperscalers are aggressively moving away from traditional copper wiring toward optical connections to slash power consumption and prevent data bottlenecks.  / / Lumentum continues to lead the AI optics pack, sitting comfortably as one of the top-performing S&P 500 stocks this year (up over 120% year-to-date).
 The Catalyst: Continuous upward revisions from Wall Street analysts and explosive earnings estimates. Current projections show Lumentum's revenue more than doubling year-over-year for the current quarter, fueled by the massive $4 billion joint strategic investment layout Nvidia made into Lumentum and Coherent ($COHR) earlier this spring.
 Why it matters: Their optical circuit switches (OCS) and co-packaged optics (CPO) solve the data-center "power bottleneck" by using light instead of electricity, drastically slashing the energy required to train massive AI clusters./

text:
$SPCX (Elon Musk) acquires Mesh

Mesh is a startup developing advanced 1.6T OSFP optical transceivers for AI and space communications

While Mesh dominates the optical engine and packaging, it relies on third party suppliers for CW DFB lasers

$SIVE specializes in high power DFB lasers and arrays for optical interconnects in AI data centers

Musk's validation increases demand for merchant suppliers like $SIVE

Which is already working on CPO projects with $POET and others

This boosts revenue and drives M&A valuations across the sector

$AAOI produces high speed fiber optic transceivers (800G+)

The photonics push by $SPCX accelerates adoption in data centers and Starlink

Elevating demand for its components among hyperscalers and improving its position in the optical supply chain

Elon's vision comes from a standpoint that none of us can comprehend, backed by insider information beyond our knowledge

Confirmations and validations like this only bring the early 2027 photonics ramp-up closer and closer

Highly bullish on $SIVE and $AAOI

$SIVE is one of the more startup friendly https://t.co/A1H7Bd6ZU4

text:
Everyone is asking which AI company will win.

I think that is the wrong question.

The real question is:

who sells the physical bottlenecks that every AI winner will be forced to consume?

Compute.
Energy.
Grid.
Cooling.
Semiconductors.
Photonics.
Silicon.
Uranium.
HALEU.
Isotopes.
Nuclear fuel.
Advanced materials.
AI may not be a bubble.
But most AI companies will die.
The internet changed the world, but most dot-com stocks disappeared.
The same can happen again.
That is why I am not only watching the obvious names like NVDA, TSM, ASML and AVGO.
I am watching the invisible infrastructure:
ASPI — isotopes, Silicon-28, HALEU
LEU — uranium enrichment / HALEU
CEG — nuclear power
BWXT — nuclear components
VRT — power and cooling
ETN — electrical infrastructure
PWR — grid construction
GEV — grid and energy systems
ALAB — AI connectivity
COHR / LITE — photonics
BESI — advanced packaging
SMR / OKLO — advanced nuclear
FLNC / POWL / AMSC — grid, storage and power equipment
ORA / FRVO — geothermal firm power
GFUZ / TAE — fusion as the long-duration frontier
I may be early.
I may be wrong on some names.
But I refuse to only chase the application layer while the real war is moving into electricity, materials, nuclear fuel, interconnects and supply chains.
The future of AI will not be decided only by models.

It will be decided by the physical infrastructure that allows intelligence to exist at scale.

I am not looking for hype.

I am looking for bottlenecks. Bye . #AIBottlenecks

#AIInfrastructure #EnergyWar
